item.title: Up For Grabs If Someone Wants It
item.content:encoded:
I've come to the conclusion that this isn't really for me. I've lost interest in it and have never really jelled with the processes and procedures for submission here - it's just not the right fit. It happens, life goes on.

If someone thinks this really needs a rewrite or has an idea that they think works better with the concept, I give full permission for them to have at it. I don't mind if it stands as is, either, but I'm not going to be doing anything more with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has some similarities to SCP-3252 but I feel like this is a better execution of the concept. But I also agree with Moose on the humanoid dinosaurs; maybe just change them to "reptilian humanoids?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've updated all the 4000s to reflect the assigned number now.

I may make some further adjustments to this now that the contest has ended, but it will require some thinking on before I do anything. I'm unlikely to revise it drastically, but I'm sure there are ways I can make this a bit stronger, I need to think on the matter before that happens though.

Suggestions are certainly welcome on that front (I may be slow responding to them, but I'll be checking). One thing I do NOT intend to do is add in a bunch of anomalous human-history events - for one, extant historical weirdnesses are great fodder for standalone SCPs (I'm trying to think of a good one around Lake Angikuni) and I don't feel that throwing them here would do them justice, and for two, I don't feel that I could effectively make up a bunch of non-extant historical weirdnesses and make it work well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article hinges on two things: (1) observation of extinction events, and (2) discussing that humanity has been eradicated.

But the extinction events aren't super interesting. It's weird to try to establish that some extinction events were caused by Generic Anomalies, and the specific details we got were underwhelming. As an example, 'What would happen if dinosaurs evolved into humanoids' has been a well-covered question outside this site, but "humanoid t-rex and triceratops" is both evolutionarily extremely unlikely and also was overdone already in the early 90's.

The discussion of humanity being previously eradicated has been done lots on this site — it's practically canon, since even before 2000. Just discussing it in a classified note is kinda 'eh', especially when the major content contributed is just to try and establish specific numerical details (# of times world has ended that Foundation knows about) that will be ignored outside this article in favor of more entertaining creative decisions. That still wouldn't be bad if it were being used as believable fluff to a stronger concept.

Consider a rework of this article post-contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good idea. I've made that change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I agree with this sentiment, I feel you should at least link the page describing the threat levels in the threat level for ease of access, like some other articles do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you still have to KNOW what level of threat is posed, because in a moment of danger you have to know what to prioritize. If your Safes can cause XK events that has to be recognized over Euclids that can't.

I guess I'd be okay with Threat Level in general if it were quantified and defined somehow. If the scale is 'won't do anything' to 'XK', then I wish we had more tiers than green/yellow/red.

Anyway, in general, those edits are enough for me to switch to a novote. I still stand by what was said about the rest of it, though — particularly the repetitive nature of the extinction event logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt like something was off with that, but I couldn't pinpoint what. Thanks for pointing that out, edit-os are being made!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough. Hopefully my next one will ring better, whenever that might be.

For the record, there are three reasons I don't intend to change this to something like that.

1) It wasn't the story I was going for with this idea. Which, okay, if it doesn't last, it doesn't last, that's how it goes sometimes. But changing it along the desired lines would essentially make it an entirely different story and thus an entirely different SCP, and I don't have a story for an entirely different SCP scripted out.

2) The site is already rife with "oh, hey, this historical event was actually anomalous" SCPs. Heaven help me if I try to actually remember all of them that have been done, lest I would step on ground that's already been trod, but beyond that, there's absolutely nothing novel about the concept, and I know how some people get when you're doing an idea that's already been done. Especially when you don't do it as well.

3) It requires a LOT of factors - interesting historical events that haven't been covered (as mentioned above), interesting ideas for anomalous activity that occurs during that time, ways of believably ensuring that the anomaly wasn't discovered (otherwise the Foundation would already know about them), and the ability to write this all in a way that seems both believable and compelling. Simply put, I don't think I'm up to that task.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this should at least say "[researcher's name] at Site 133 placed an order" rather than just "the Foundation ordered one". It's already hard to believe that they'd just put a classified facility as their mailing address instead of a safehouse with a street address or something. Or a front company with a street address. This just seems unrealistic.

You're right about that, that didn't occur to me. Edit-os shall be had! I'll make the delivery to a front company and mention that "an order was placed" at some level of authorization.

I'm still torn on Threat Level. On the one hand, I like the addition of another cool-sounding category with sci-fi flare in SCP articles. On the other hand, I always liked that the Foundation didn't bother with assessing the severity of threat(s) posed by an anomaly because the only thing that matters to them is containment, and containment difficulty is objective while the severity of a threat can be pretty subjective.

See, that's something I find unrealistic. Yes, the Foundation is concerned with containment regardless of the threat posed by an object, but you still have to KNOW what level of threat is posed, because in a moment of danger you have to know what to prioritize. If your Safes can cause XK events that has to be recognized over Euclids that can't. That's also the reason Object Class itself isn't a good enough mechanism, unless a lot of new definitions were applied to Object class to cover the same information as threat levels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Science quibble:

"The unit has a single opening leading into an interior chamber filled with an unknown liquid with buoyancy slightly higher than water, such that subjects reliably float within it."

This should be either "liquid providing buoyancy slightly greater than water", or "liquid with density slightly greater than water". If the liquid had a buoyancy greater than water, it would float on water and humans wouldn't float as well in it. (has vs. provides) Semantics, but that's what science is about! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding Conwell in full. Also:

The Foundation placed an order for one unit prior to enacting removal of the listing. 24 hours after placement of the order, a box containing an instance of SCP-4000 was discovered outside Site 133.

I feel like this should at least say "[researcher's name] at Site 133 placed an order" rather than just "the Foundation ordered one". It's already hard to believe that they'd just put a classified facility as their mailing address instead of a safehouse with a street address or something. Or a front company with a street address. This just seems unrealistic.

I admire the effort put into this, especially as a first SCP, but there are definitely some issues I can't ignore.

I'm still torn on Threat Level. On the one hand, I like the addition of another cool-sounding category with sci-fi flare in SCP articles. On the other hand, I always liked that the Foundation didn't bother with assessing the severity of threat(s) posed by an anomaly because the only thing that matters to them is containment, and containment difficulty is objective while the severity of a threat can be pretty subjective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be blunt, I am rather underwhelmed.

The object itself is rather interesting, a window to the past that can let you see major events and confirm theories. There is potential there for interesting storytelling and experimentation. However, the test log is just various flavors of observing extinction events which are rather bland after the first few, and you expunge the one that seems like it would be the most interesting. You could have tapped into a this to offer your own take on a lot of historical phenomena and mysteries, but instead, we just get different flavors of extinction events.

Additionally, the tie into SCP-2000 is nothing that hasn't been explored fairly well in that very skip, and just kind of dries up the last bit of momentum the article had going for it.

-1. Solid concept, solid set up, didn't quite stick the landing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Totally get where you're coming from - I can say you definitely don't overstay your welcome on this entry. Looking forward to seeing more of what you write!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for sharing! I definitely understand the idea of "show, don't tell" as it applies here, and while admittedly I wasn't approaching it from a 'mystery' perspective, I can see how that could add to it. I'm also wary, though, of a common complaint I've seen in the comments of other SCPs about excessive length, especially when it comes to addenda. While the note doesn't exactly draw out the mystery, it does get to the point much more quickly than a set of experiment logs would have. It's hard to know what balance to strike, and once I get more experience I might have a better sense of what I can get away with in terms of content quantity without losing readers. But I appreciate your input and I'll definitely keep that in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this conceptually; for a first skip, it's pretty good. The tie in with 2000 might be unpopular as some don't like crosslinks, but for this concept it's almost inevitable. I do think this kind of peters out at the final note - I guess I don't like being told about a thing that happened where there could be logs that "show it" to me. So when he talks about the analysis of the artifacts, but there's no adventure into the timestream that we get to see, it's kind of an anticlimax rather than a big reveal. There's no mystery or puzzle - it's all laid out for me. No-vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Threat level sees only sporadic use but I rather like it, because object class doesn't always capture the true level of danger - it's more of a "how easy is this to contain" thing, and there are things that are easy to contain but extremely dangerous if they were ever stolen/released in a containment breach. There is a Threat Level page which tells you what each color means, so I think that it should be okay (and only by getting more use will people get an intuitive sense of what each level means - it's not like Euclid and Keter are intuitive at first glance either.)

For those who aren't fond of it, it's not like it replaces Object Class, which is still there. So, everyone should be happy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The threat level is unnecessary. The object class is more direct with what kind of skip it is and is more familiar. I doubt most people know what Threat Level Green means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it wise to make a contest entry my first SCP submission? Maybe not, but I had the idea and the feedback was promising. Thank you to Gabriel Jade for initial feedback, and I'm happy to listen to any other feedback anyone wants to offer.
